canonical_definition: Across the provided literature, AMC 2023 is consistently used as a measurement instrument to evaluate mathematical reasoning in language models. It is variously referred to as a "benchmark," "dataset," or "exam," with all definitions agreeing that it consists of problems from the 2023 American Mathematics Competitions. The problems are characterized as being at the high school level and are frequently described as "challenging" or "competition-level." As one paper notes, newer datasets like it "represent higher levels of difficulty." The primary construct it is used to assess is mathematical reasoning, with some papers specifying "advanced arithmetic reasoning" or "competition-level mathematical reasoning." In evaluation settings, AMC 2023 is frequently used alongside other mathematical benchmarks such as AIME24, MATH-500, and Olympiad Bench to assess model performance on difficult problem-solving tasks.
papers: Beyond Similarity: A Gradient-based Graph Method for Instruction Tuning Data Selection
SEA: Low-Resource Safety Alignment for Multimodal Large Language Models via Synthetic Embeddings
The Essence of Contextual Understanding in Theory of Mind: A Study on Question Answering with Story Characters
Turning Trash into Treasure: Accelerating Inference of Large Language Models with Token Recycling
GATEAU: Selecting Influential Samples for Long Context Alignment
SATER: A Self-Aware and Token-Efficient Approach to Routing and Cascading
Seeing is Believing, but How Much? A Comprehensive Analysis of Verbalized Calibration in Vision-Language Models
BRiTE: Bootstrapping Reinforced Thinking Process to Enhance Language Model Reasoning
Leveraging Online Olympiad-Level Math Problems for LLMs Training and Contamination-Resistant Evaluation
Satori: Reinforcement Learning with Chain-of-Action-Thought Enhances LLM Reasoning via Autoregressive Search
rStar-Math: Small LLMs Can Master Math Reasoning with Self-Evolved Deep Thinking

definitions.text: A benchmark consisting of problems from the 2023 American Mathematics Competitions, used to evaluate competition-level mathematical reasoning.

relationships.paper: OpenMathInstruct-2: Accelerating AI for Math with Massive Open-Source Instruction Data
relationships.evidence: For the in-domain scenario, we evaluate our method on several challenging open-source mathematical benchmarks, including MATHOAI (Lightman et al., 2024), AIME2024, and AMC2023. (Section 5.1)
